text: The surgeon retracts the heavy colon and lifts it towards the anterior abdominal wall to locate the inferior mesenteric pedicle. Dissection begins laterally, aiming to identify the mesorectum and bring the dissection proximally, navigating through thickened mesentery due to previous diverticulitis attacks.

The surgeon uses a lighted stent to guide the dissection around the left ureter, which is affected by inflammation. Dr. Crean assists with suction to clear the field, while the surgeon employs scissors and minimal electrocautery to carefully dissect and identify the normal anatomical planes. CO2 insufflation aids in separating the tissue planes.

The surgeon continues dissection around the superior hemorrhoidal vein and artery, carefully separating the surrounding adipose tissue to isolate each vessel individually. The area shows significant inflammation, and extreme care is taken due to the proximity of the left ureter to these vessels. The lighted stents are not visible in the background, indicating they have been lifted, necessitating careful identification of the ureter before proceeding with pedicle dissection.

The surgeon selects a point at the proximal rectum and distal sigmoid colon, using a vessel sealer to clear the mesorectum for improved hemostasis. The mesentery of the proximal descending colon is taken to prepare for colorectal anastomosis. Indocyanine green (ICG) is administered to assess perfusion, showing excellent blood flow distally and proximally. Rectal dilators are introduced to determine the appropriate size for the EA stapler.

The surgeon decides to use a 29 EA stapler for the procedure. A colotomy is made to pass the anvil, which is then connected to the stapler spike through the twelfth port. The proximal rectum is stapled, but the tissue is initially too thick for the green load stapler to clamp and fire. Additional cleaning of lateral attachments is performed to facilitate stapler firing.

The surgeon attaches the spike of the anvil to the anvil itself and creates another colotomy. The spike is passed more proximally, and the distal portion of the colon is stapled off to create an end-to-side anastomosis between the end of the rectum and the side of the descending colon. The surgeon carefully maneuvers the spike, ensuring it does not puncture the tinea, and retrieves it completely before stapling the distal colon.

After creating the anastomosis, the surgeon observes a small portion of the descending colon with an exposed staple line and places bicoral sutures at the site. The vessel sealer is used as a needle holder for suturing. A flexible endoscopy and air leak test are performed to inspect the lumen and ensure the staple line is intact, with no bleeding observed. A small fan and steel incision is made to extract the large specimen.
